title: Blood Transfusion — Administration
oneLiner: Cross-matched blood components given to correct anaemia, haemorrhage, or coagulopathy — requires strict identity checks, baseline vitals, and close monitoring for life-threatening reactions.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute haemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO incompatibility):** Immediate pain at infusion site/flank, hypotension, dark urine — stop transfusion *before* you check anything else.
- **Bacterial contamination (platelets most common):** High fever, rigors, marked hypotension within 15-30 minutes of starting — stop infusion, take blood cultures, call transfusion lab.
- **Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI):** Acute dyspnoea, hypoxia, bilateral crackles *without* signs of fluid overload (JVP normal, no peripheral oedema) — stop transfusion, give high-flow oxygen, call senior immediately.
sections.Presentation (of the patient needing tra:
- **Symptoms:** Fatigue, dyspnoea on exertion, palpitations, dizziness (anaemia); overt bleeding (haematemesis, melaena, trauma); bruising or oozing from sites (coagulopathy).
- **Key exam findings:** Pallor, tachycardia, hypotension (if acute blood loss), petechiae/ecchymoses (platelet deficiency), jaundice (haemolysis), signs of decompensated heart failure (fluid overload risk).
sections.Differential Diagnosis (of the patient’s:
- **Febrile non-haemolytic reaction (most common)** – temp rise <1°C, no hypotension, no respiratory distress → treat with paracetamol and slow rate.
- **Allergic reaction** – urticaria, pruritus, wheeze (mild), or anaphylaxis (severe) → stop, give antihistamine, if severe IM adrenaline.
- **Acute haemolytic reaction** – pain, hypotension, dark urine → stop, re-check identity, send lab samples.
- **Bacterial contamination** – high fever, rigors, hypotension → stop, blood cultures, notify microbiology.
- **TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload)** – dyspnoea, raised JVP, ↑ BP, crackles → stop, sit patient up, furosemide.
- **TRALI** – hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates, no signs of volume overload → stop, oxygen, senior review.
- **Hypocalcaemia** – perioral tingling, muscle cramps, prolonged QT (massive transfusion) → give IV calcium gluconate [check local policy].
- **Hyperkalaemia** – ECG changes (peaked T waves), arrhythmias (especially in stored blood) → treat per local potassium protocol.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Patient ID check (Name, DOB, hospital number) – repeat *immediately* before connecting blood.
    - Baseline vital signs (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature) – document on transfusion chart.
    - If reaction suspected: *stop transfusion, keep IV access, measure vitals every 5 min.*
- **Bloods:**
    - Pre-transfusion: FBC, group & screen, coagulation if coagulopathy.
    - If reaction: repeat group & sample (for direct antiglobulin test / crossmatch), FBC, U&E, LDH, bilirubin, haptoglobin, blood cultures, coagulation screen.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. If TACO/TRALI suspected: chest X-ray (bilateral opacities in TRALI vs cardiomegaly/fluid in TACO).
- **Special tests:** Bacterial culture of the transfusion bag (if contamination suspected).
- **Diagnostic scoring system:** Not for administration. BCSH guidelines for transfusion reactions.
- **Never-miss test:** **Second patient identity check** (armband vs documentation vs blood bag compatibility form) – failure to do this is the commonest cause of fatal ABO mismatch.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ward):**
    - **Pre-administration:**
        - Check valid consent (verbal in emergency, written if elective).
        - Ensure IV access (18-20G for red cells, 20-22G for platelets/plasma; larger if rapid transfusion).
        - Check blood component against patient ID with **two staff members** (one RN, one doctor or second RN) – bag label, compatibility form, patient armband.
        - Document baseline observations.
    - **During transfusion:**
        - Rate for red cells: first 15 min at 1ml/kg/hr (max 100 ml/hr), then increase to 2-4 ml/kg/hr if stable.
        - Monitoring: visual check every 5 min for first 15 min, then every 15 min minimum throughout.
        - Stop transfusion if any symptom/sign of reaction — call for help.
    - **If reaction occurs:**
        - **Stop immediately.** Keep IV line open (0.9% saline).
        - Assess airway, breathing, circulation.
        - Measure vital signs every 5 min.
        - Return blood bag and giving set to transfusion lab for investigation.
        - Take blood samples (as above).
        - Treat per cause: antihistamine/fever → slow rate + paracetamol; anaphylaxis → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000); TACO → furosemide 20-40 mg IV; TRALI → high-flow oxygen, ICU referral.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Continue routine monitoring per local policy (e.g., FBC post-transfusion if no reaction).
    - Ensure documentation of reaction in hospital incident reporting system.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for acute administration.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV access:** maintain 20G cannula until transfusion completed.
    - **Observation frequency:** baseline → q5min for first 15 min, then q15min if stable.
    - **Fluids:** no routine IV fluids unless hypovolaemic; 0.9% saline to keep vein open if reaction.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** continue if no contraindication (risk of bleeding from coagulopathy? assess).
    - **Diet/NBM:** no restriction.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **For febrile non-haemolytic reaction:** Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV (single dose).
    - **For allergic urticaria/mild reaction:** Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV (slow bolus) or loratadine 10 mg PO.
    - **For anaphylaxis:** Adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg IM (1:1000) into anterolateral thigh — repeated q5 min if no improvement.
    - **For TACO:** Furosemide (frusemide) 20-40 mg IV (slow bolus); can repeat after 2 hours.
- **Common alternatives:** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV for severe allergic reaction (less immediate than adrenaline).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Chlorphenamine: caution in glaucoma, prostate hypertrophy.
    - Furosemide: avoid if hypovolaemic (precipitate hypotension).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Furosemide – dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl <30: reduce dose or use alternative).
    - No adjustment needed for paracetamol or chlorphenamine except severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IM adrenaline IV** – dilutes concentration; use 1:1000 for IM, 1:10,000 for IV (only in cardiac arrest setting). Also: **Furosemide given too fast** – can cause ototoxicity; max infusion rate 4 mg/min for IV bolus.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Acute haemolytic reaction (ABO mismatch) → DIC, AKI, shock.
    - Febrile non-haemolytic reaction → self-limiting but can mimic serious.
    - Allergic / anaphylactic reaction.
    - Bacterial contamination → sepsis.
    - TACO → pulmonary oedema.
    - TRALI → acute respiratory distress.
    - Hypocalcaemia / hyperkalaemia (massive transfusion).
- **Chronic:**
    - Iron overload (frequent transfusion e.g. sickle cell, thalassaemia) → check ferritin, consider chelation.
    - Alloimmunisation (platelet refractoriness, haemolytic reactions in future).
    - Transfusion-transmitted infections (rare in UK/Ireland).
sections.Prognosis: - Worsening of underlying disease if transfusion withheld. Most reactions are non-fatal if detected early. TRALI mortality 10-20%; acute haemolytic reaction mortality 5-10% (higher if delayed recognition).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a formal two-person identity check** — it prevents 99% of ABO mismatches.
- **Give the first 50 mL of any unit slowly** (over 15 min) — this is the window to detect the majority of fatal reactions.
- **If a patient has a prior history of febrile reaction, pre-medicate with paracetamol 1 g PO 1 hour before** (but still monitor closely).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t rely on a verbal check alone** — the most common error is not comparing armband with bag label.
- **Don’t delay stopping the transfusion** when symptoms appear — waiting for senior permission wastes time.
- **Don’t restart the same bag** after a suspected reaction — keep it for testing and never re-infuse.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home Aft:
- **Return if:** Fever, chills, jaundice, dark urine, shortness of breath, or bleeding (within 24 hours of transfusion).
- **Follow-up:** No routine; GP notified if elective transfusion. For reaction, transfusion lab will flag for future components.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
    - Any sign of acute haemolytic reaction (pain, hypotension, dark urine).
    - Suspected TRALI (acute hypoxia with bilateral infiltrates).
    - Anaphylaxis not responding to IM adrenaline.
    - NEWS2 ≥ 5 during transfusion.
- **Call ICU if:**
    - Persistent hypoxia (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2) or need for non-invasive ventilation.
    - Haemodynamic instability despite fluid challenge (crystalloid 500 mL).
    - Ongoing massive transfusion requirement (e.g., 4+ units red cells in 1 hour).

rawMarkdown:
## Blood Transfusion — Administration

**One-liner:** Cross-matched blood components given to correct anaemia, haemorrhage, or coagulopathy — requires strict identity checks, baseline vitals, and close monitoring for life-threatening reactions.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute haemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO incompatibility):** Immediate pain at infusion site/flank, hypotension, dark urine — stop transfusion *before* you check anything else.
- **Bacterial contamination (platelets most common):** High fever, rigors, marked hypotension within 15-30 minutes of starting — stop infusion, take blood cultures, call transfusion lab.
- **Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI):** Acute dyspnoea, hypoxia, bilateral crackles *without* signs of fluid overload (JVP normal, no peripheral oedema) — stop transfusion, give high-flow oxygen, call senior immediately.

### Presentation (of the patient needing transfusion — i.e. what you see on the ward)
- **Symptoms:** Fatigue, dyspnoea on exertion, palpitations, dizziness (anaemia); overt bleeding (haematemesis, melaena, trauma); bruising or oozing from sites (coagulopathy).
- **Key exam findings:** Pallor, tachycardia, hypotension (if acute blood loss), petechiae/ecchymoses (platelet deficiency), jaundice (haemolysis), signs of decompensated heart failure (fluid overload risk).

### Differential Diagnosis (of the patient’s condition that triggers transfusion — not listed here in detail; focus on the *transfusion* itself. If you are called to a patient receiving blood who deteriorates, consider these causes of acute deterioration during transfusion):
- **Febrile non-haemolytic reaction (most common)** – temp rise <1°C, no hypotension, no respiratory distress → treat with paracetamol and slow rate.
- **Allergic reaction** – urticaria, pruritus, wheeze (mild), or anaphylaxis (severe) → stop, give antihistamine, if severe IM adrenaline.
- **Acute haemolytic reaction** – pain, hypotension, dark urine → stop, re-check identity, send lab samples.
- **Bacterial contamination** – high fever, rigors, hypotension → stop, blood cultures, notify microbiology.
- **TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload)** – dyspnoea, raised JVP, ↑ BP, crackles → stop, sit patient up, furosemide.
- **TRALI** – hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates, no signs of volume overload → stop, oxygen, senior review.
- **Hypocalcaemia** – perioral tingling, muscle cramps, prolonged QT (massive transfusion) → give IV calcium gluconate [check local policy].
- **Hyperkalaemia** – ECG changes (peaked T waves), arrhythmias (especially in stored blood) → treat per local potassium protocol.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Patient ID check (Name, DOB, hospital number) – repeat *immediately* before connecting blood.
    - Baseline vital signs (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature) – document on transfusion chart.
    - If reaction suspected: *stop transfusion, keep IV access, measure vitals every 5 min.*
- **Bloods:**
    - Pre-transfusion: FBC, group & screen, coagulation if coagulopathy.
    - If reaction: repeat group & sample (for direct antiglobulin test / crossmatch), FBC, U&E, LDH, bilirubin, haptoglobin, blood cultures, coagulation screen.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. If TACO/TRALI suspected: chest X-ray (bilateral opacities in TRALI vs cardiomegaly/fluid in TACO).
- **Special tests:** Bacterial culture of the transfusion bag (if contamination suspected).
- **Diagnostic scoring system:** Not for administration. BCSH guidelines for transfusion reactions.
- **Never-miss test:** **Second patient identity check** (armband vs documentation vs blood bag compatibility form) – failure to do this is the commonest cause of fatal ABO mismatch.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ward):**
    - **Pre-administration:**
        - Check valid consent (verbal in emergency, written if elective).
        - Ensure IV access (18-20G for red cells, 20-22G for platelets/plasma; larger if rapid transfusion).
        - Check blood component against patient ID with **two staff members** (one RN, one doctor or second RN) – bag label, compatibility form, patient armband.
        - Document baseline observations.
    - **During transfusion:**
        - Rate for red cells: first 15 min at 1ml/kg/hr (max 100 ml/hr), then increase to 2-4 ml/kg/hr if stable.
        - Monitoring: visual check every 5 min for first 15 min, then every 15 min minimum throughout.
        - Stop transfusion if any symptom/sign of reaction — call for help.
    - **If reaction occurs:**
        - **Stop immediately.** Keep IV line open (0.9% saline).
        - Assess airway, breathing, circulation.
        - Measure vital signs every 5 min.
        - Return blood bag and giving set to transfusion lab for investigation.
        - Take blood samples (as above).
        - Treat per cause: antihistamine/fever → slow rate + paracetamol; anaphylaxis → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000); TACO → furosemide 20-40 mg IV; TRALI → high-flow oxygen, ICU referral.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Continue routine monitoring per local policy (e.g., FBC post-transfusion if no reaction).
    - Ensure documentation of reaction in hospital incident reporting system.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for acute administration.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV access:** maintain 20G cannula until transfusion completed.
    - **Observation frequency:** baseline → q5min for first 15 min, then q15min if stable.
    - **Fluids:** no routine IV fluids unless hypovolaemic; 0.9% saline to keep vein open if reaction.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** continue if no contraindication (risk of bleeding from coagulopathy? assess).
    - **Diet/NBM:** no restriction.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **For febrile non-haemolytic reaction:** Paracetamol 1 g PO/IV (single dose).
    - **For allergic urticaria/mild reaction:** Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV (slow bolus) or loratadine 10 mg PO.
    - **For anaphylaxis:** Adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg IM (1:1000) into anterolateral thigh — repeated q5 min if no improvement.
    - **For TACO:** Furosemide (frusemide) 20-40 mg IV (slow bolus); can repeat after 2 hours.
- **Common alternatives:** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV for severe allergic reaction (less immediate than adrenaline).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Chlorphenamine: caution in glaucoma, prostate hypertrophy.
    - Furosemide: avoid if hypovolaemic (precipitate hypotension).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Furosemide – dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl <30: reduce dose or use alternative).
    - No adjustment needed for paracetamol or chlorphenamine except severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IM adrenaline IV** – dilutes concentration; use 1:1000 for IM, 1:10,000 for IV (only in cardiac arrest setting). Also: **Furosemide given too fast** – can cause ototoxicity; max infusion rate 4 mg/min for IV bolus.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Acute haemolytic reaction (ABO mismatch) → DIC, AKI, shock.
    - Febrile non-haemolytic reaction → self-limiting but can mimic serious.
    - Allergic / anaphylactic reaction.
    - Bacterial contamination → sepsis.
    - TACO → pulmonary oedema.
    - TRALI → acute respiratory distress.
    - Hypocalcaemia / hyperkalaemia (massive transfusion).
- **Chronic:**
    - Iron overload (frequent transfusion e.g. sickle cell, thalassaemia) → check ferritin, consider chelation.
    - Alloimmunisation (platelet refractoriness, haemolytic reactions in future).
    - Transfusion-transmitted infections (rare in UK/Ireland).

### Prognosis
- Worsening of underlying disease if transfusion withheld. Most reactions are non-fatal if detected early. TRALI mortality 10-20%; acute haemolytic reaction mortality 5-10% (higher if delayed recognition).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a formal two-person identity check** — it prevents 99% of ABO mismatches.
- **Give the first 50 mL of any unit slowly** (over 15 min) — this is the window to detect the majority of fatal reactions.
- **If a patient has a prior history of febrile reaction, pre-medicate with paracetamol 1 g PO 1 hour before** (but still monitor closely).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t rely on a verbal check alone** — the most common error is not comparing armband with bag label.
- **Don’t delay stopping the transfusion** when symptoms appear — waiting for senior permission wastes time.
- **Don’t restart the same bag** after a suspected reaction — keep it for testing and never re-infuse.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home After Transfusion)
- **Return if:** Fever, chills, jaundice, dark urine, shortness of breath, or bleeding (within 24 hours of transfusion).
- **Follow-up:** No routine; GP notified if elective transfusion. For reaction, transfusion lab will flag for future components.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
    - Any sign of acute haemolytic reaction (pain, hypotension, dark urine).
    - Suspected TRALI (acute hypoxia with bilateral infiltrates).
    - Anaphylaxis not responding to IM adrenaline.
    - NEWS2 ≥ 5 during transfusion.
- **Call ICU if:**
    - Persistent hypoxia (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2) or need for non-invasive ventilation.
    - Haemodynamic instability despite fluid challenge (crystalloid 500 mL).
    - Ongoing massive transfusion requirement (e.g., 4+ units red cells in 1 hour).
